NAME

module_utilities - Module_utilities

SYNOPSIS

Files


file time.c
⏱️ High-precision timing utilities implementation file time.h
⏱️ High-precision timing utilities using sokol_time.h and uthash

Convert struct timespec to nanoseconds. void time_ns_to_timespec (uint64_t ns, struct timespec *ts)
Convert nanoseconds to struct timespec.

Detailed Description

This module provides a simple timing API for performance measurement:

  • START_TIMER("name", ...) - Begin timing with formatted message
  • STOP_TIMER("name") - End timing, log result, return elapsed time

Features:

  • Cross-platform high-resolution timing (sokol_time.h)
  • Automatic hashtable management for named timers
  • Thread-safe operation with mutex protection
  • Formatted timer names with printf-style arguments
  • Zero overhead when timing is disabled

Example usage:

START_TIMER("process_frame_%d", frame_num);
// ... do work ...
double elapsed_ns = STOP_TIMER("process_frame_%d", frame_num);
// Use time_pretty() for human-readable formatted output
char duration_str[32];
time_pretty((uint64_t)elapsed_ns, -1, duration_str, sizeof(duration_str));
log_info("Frame took %s", duration_str);

Author

Zachary Fogg me@zfo.gg

Date

September 2025

#define START_TIMER( name_fmt, ...)

#include <include/ascii-chat/util/time.h>Value:

do {                                                                                                                 \


  if (timer_is_initialized()) {                                                                                      \


    char _timer_name_buf[256];                                                                                       \


    char _timer_name_with_tid[280];                                                                                  \


    snprintf(_timer_name_buf, sizeof(_timer_name_buf), name_fmt, ##__VA_ARGS__);                                     \


    snprintf(_timer_name_with_tid, sizeof(_timer_name_with_tid), "%s_tid%llu", _timer_name_buf,                      \


             (unsigned long long)asciichat_thread_current_id());                                                     \


    (void)timer_start(_timer_name_with_tid);                                                                         \


  }                                                                                                                  \
} while (0)




Start a timer with formatted name. The timer name is created by
    formatting the name_fmt string with the provided arguments. This complete
    formatted string becomes the unique key in the hashtable.




IMPORTANT: Timer names must be unique. If a timer with the same
    formatted name already exists, timer_start() will return false and
    log an error.




Usage: START_TIMER("lock_%p", lock_ptr); // Unique per
    lock address START_TIMER("process_frame_%d", frame_id); // Unique
    per frame START_TIMER("client_%u_decode", client_id); // Unique
    per client




Parameters


name_fmt Printf-style format string for timer name


... Format arguments to create unique key

Start a timer without thread ID (for cross-thread timers). Use
    this variant when a timer may be stopped on a different thread than it was
    started. The timer name will NOT include thread ID, so it must be globally
    unique through other means (e.g., including a unique identifier in the
    format string).




Parameters


name_fmt Printf-style format string for timer name


... Format arguments to create unique key

#define
  STOP_TIMER( name_fmt, ...)


#include
    <include/ascii-chat/util/time.h>Value:


({                                                                                                                   \


  double _elapsed = -1.0;                                                                                            \


  if (timer_is_initialized()) {                                                                                      \


    char _timer_name_buf[256];                                                                                       \


    char _timer_name_with_tid[280];                                                                                  \

             (unsigned long long)asciichat_thread_current_id());                                                     \


    _elapsed = timer_stop(_timer_name_with_tid);                                                                     \


  }                                                                                                                  \


  _elapsed;                                                                                                          \
})






Stop a timer with formatted name and return elapsed time. The
    timer name must match exactly (including all format arguments) with the name
    used in START_TIMER(). The formatted string is used as the hashtable
    key lookup.




Usage: double ns = STOP_TIMER("lock_%p", lock_ptr);
    double ns = STOP_TIMER("process_frame_%d", frame_id);




Parameters


name_fmt Printf-style format string for timer name
  (must match START_TIMER)


... Format arguments (must match START_TIMER to create same key)


Returns


Elapsed time in nanoseconds, or -1.0 if timer not
  found

#define
  STOP_TIMER_AND_LOG( log_level, threshold_ns, timer_name, msg_fmt,
  ...)


#include
    <include/ascii-chat/util/time.h>Value:


do {                                                                                                                 \


  double _elapsed_ns = STOP_TIMER(timer_name, ##__VA_ARGS__);                                                        \


  if (_elapsed_ns >= 0.0 && (threshold_ns == 0 || _elapsed_ns >= (double)(threshold_ns))) {                          \


    char _duration_str[32];                                                                                          \


    time_pretty((uint64_t)_elapsed_ns, -1, _duration_str, sizeof(_duration_str));                                    \


    log_##log_level(msg_fmt " in %s", ##__VA_ARGS__, _duration_str);                                                 \


  }                                                                                                                  \
} while (0)






Stop a timer and log the result with a custom message. Combines
    STOP_TIMER() with logging. The timer is stopped, elapsed time is
    retrieved, and a log message is generated with the elapsed time appended in
    human-readable format. Optionally filters based on a minimum elapsed time
    threshold.




Usage: STOP_TIMER_AND_LOG(info, 0, "client_handshake",
    "Crypto handshake completed successfully");
    STOP_TIMER_AND_LOG(debug, 5000000, "process_frame_%d", "Frame
    %d processed", frame_id, frame_id); STOP_TIMER_AND_LOG(dev,
    NS_PER_MS_INT, "render", "Rendering complete");




The macro will append " in X.XXms" (or appropriate unit)
    to your message automatically. Supported log levels: dev, debug, info, warn,
    error, fatal




Parameters


log_level Log level name (dev, debug, info, warn,
  error, fatal - without log_ prefix)


threshold_ns Minimum elapsed time in nanoseconds to trigger log (0 =
  always log)


timer_name Timer name (must match START_TIMER call)


msg_fmt Printf-style format string for the message


... Format arguments for both timer name and message


Definition at line 612 of file time.h.


612 #define STOP_TIMER_AND_LOG(log_level, threshold_ns, timer_name, msg_fmt, ...)                                          \
613   do {                                                                                                                 \
614     double _elapsed_ns = STOP_TIMER(timer_name, ##__VA_ARGS__);                                                        \
615     if (_elapsed_ns >= 0.0 && (threshold_ns == 0 || _elapsed_ns >= (double)(threshold_ns))) {                          \
616       char _duration_str[32];                                                                                          \
617       time_pretty((uint64_t)_elapsed_ns, -1, _duration_str, sizeof(_duration_str));                                    \
618       log_##log_level(msg_fmt " in %s", ##__VA_ARGS__, _duration_str);                                                 \
619     }                                                                                                                  \
620   } while (0)

#define
  STOP_TIMER_AND_LOG_EVERY( log_level, interval_ns, threshold_ns, timer_name,
  msg_fmt, ...)


#include
    <include/ascii-chat/util/time.h>Value:


do {                                                                                                                 \


  double _elapsed_ns = STOP_TIMER(timer_name, ##__VA_ARGS__);                                                        \


  if (_elapsed_ns >= 0.0 && (threshold_ns == 0 || _elapsed_ns >= (double)(threshold_ns))) {                          \


    static atomic_t _log_every_last_time = {0};                                                                      \


    uint64_t _log_every_now = time_get_ns();                                                                         \


    uint64_t _log_every_last = atomic_load_u64(&_log_every_last_time);                                               \


    if (_log_every_now - _log_every_last >= (uint64_t)(interval_ns)) {                                               \


      if (atomic_cas_u64(&_log_every_last_time, &_log_every_last, _log_every_now)) {                                 \


        char _duration_str[32];                                                                                      \


        time_pretty((uint64_t)_elapsed_ns, -1, _duration_str, sizeof(_duration_str));                                \


        log_##log_level(msg_fmt " in %s", ##__VA_ARGS__, _duration_str);                                             \


      }                                                                                                              \


    }                                                                                                                \


  }                                                                                                                  \
} while (0)






Stop a timer and log the result with rate limiting. Combines
    STOP_TIMER() with rate-limited logging. The timer is stopped, elapsed
    time is retrieved, and a log message is generated only if:



  
1.

  
The elapsed time exceeds the threshold (if non-zero), AND

  
2.

  
The specified time interval has passed since the last logged
    occurrence




This prevents log spam from frequent operations while also
    filtering out fast operations.




Usage: STOP_TIMER_AND_LOG_EVERY(dev, 1000000000, 0,
    "opus_encode", "Opus encode completed");
    STOP_TIMER_AND_LOG_EVERY(info, 5000000000, NS_PER_MS_INT,
    "process_frame_%d", "Frame %d processed", frame_id);




The macro will append " in X.XXms" (or appropriate unit)
    to your message automatically. Supported log levels: dev, debug, info, warn,
    error, fatal




Parameters


log_level Log level name (dev, debug, info, warn,
  error, fatal - without log_ prefix)


interval_ns Time interval in nanoseconds between log emissions (rate
  limiting)


threshold_ns Minimum elapsed time in nanoseconds to trigger log (0 = no
  threshold)


timer_name Timer name (must match START_TIMER call)


msg_fmt Printf-style format string for the message


... Format arguments for both timer name and message


Definition at line 647 of file time.h.


647 #define STOP_TIMER_AND_LOG_EVERY(log_level, interval_ns, threshold_ns, timer_name, msg_fmt, ...)                       \
648   do {                                                                                                                 \
649     double _elapsed_ns = STOP_TIMER(timer_name, ##__VA_ARGS__);                                                        \
650     if (_elapsed_ns >= 0.0 && (threshold_ns == 0 || _elapsed_ns >= (double)(threshold_ns))) {                          \
651       static atomic_t _log_every_last_time = {0};                                                                      \
652       uint64_t _log_every_now = time_get_ns();                                                                         \
653       uint64_t _log_every_last = atomic_load_u64(&_log_every_last_time);                                               \
654       if (_log_every_now - _log_every_last >= (uint64_t)(interval_ns)) {                                               \
655         if (atomic_cas_u64(&_log_every_last_time, &_log_every_last, _log_every_now)) {                                 \
656           char _duration_str[32];                                                                                      \
657           time_pretty((uint64_t)_elapsed_ns, -1, _duration_str, sizeof(_duration_str));                                \
658           log_##log_level(msg_fmt " in %s", ##__VA_ARGS__, _duration_str);                                             \
659         }                                                                                                              \
660       }                                                                                                                \
661     }                                                                                                                  \
662   } while (0)

Stop a cross-thread timer and return elapsed time. Use this
    variant when stopping a timer started with START_TIMER_GLOBAL. The timer
    name must match exactly (without thread ID appended).




Parameters


name_fmt Printf-style format string for timer name
  (must match START_TIMER_GLOBAL)


... Format arguments (must match START_TIMER_GLOBAL to create same
  key)


Returns


Elapsed time in nanoseconds, or -1.0 if timer not
  found

uint64_t adaptive_sleep_calculate
  (adaptive_sleep_state_t * state, size_t queue_depth, size_t
  target_depth)


#include <include/ascii-chat/util/time.h>


Calculate adaptive sleep time based on queue depth. Evaluates
    current queue depth against target and adjusts the speed multiplier:


    
  
  • If queue_depth > target_depth: speed up (reduce sleep, drain queue
      faster)
    • 
  
  • If queue_depth < target_depth: slow down (increase sleep back to
      baseline)
    • 
  
  • Speed multiplier changes gradually based on
    speedup_rate/slowdown_rate
    • 



The actual sleep time is calculated as: baseline_sleep_ns /
    current_speed_multiplier




Example usage:




adaptive_sleep_state_t sleep_state;
adaptive_sleep_config_t config = {


  .baseline_sleep_ns = 16666667,  // ~60 FPS (16.67ms)


  .min_speed_multiplier = 1.0,    // Never slower than baseline


  .max_speed_multiplier = 4.0,    // Can process up to 4x faster (240 FPS)


  .speedup_rate = 0.1,            // Ramp up 10% per frame


  .slowdown_rate = 0.05           // Ramp down 5% per frame
};
adaptive_sleep_init(&sleep_state, &config);
while (running) {


  // Process one frame/packet/unit of work


  process_data();


  // Sleep adaptively based on queue depth


  size_t queue_depth = get_queue_size();


  uint64_t sleep_ns = adaptive_sleep_calculate(&sleep_state, queue_depth, 10);


  platform_sleep_us(sleep_ns / 1000);
}



Parameters


state Pointer to adaptive sleep state (will be
  updated)


queue_depth Current number of items in queue/buffer


target_depth Desired queue depth (0 = empty queue target)


Returns


Sleep time in nanoseconds


Definition at line 624 of file util/time.c.


624                                                                                                           {
625   if (!state) {
626     return 0;
627   }
628 
629   const adaptive_sleep_config_t *cfg = &state->config;
630 
631   // Determine if we need to speed up or slow down
632   double desired_multiplier;
633 
634   if (queue_depth > target_depth) {
635     // Queue is building up - speed up processing
636     // The more items in queue, the faster we should process
637     size_t backlog = queue_depth - target_depth;
638 
639     // Calculate desired speed based on backlog
640     // Each item above target increases speed proportionally
641     // This ensures we drain the queue faster the more backed up we are
642     double backlog_factor = 1.0 + ((double)backlog / (double)(target_depth + 1));
643     desired_multiplier = cfg->min_speed_multiplier * backlog_factor;
644 
645     // Clamp to max speed
646     if (desired_multiplier > cfg->max_speed_multiplier) {
647       desired_multiplier = cfg->max_speed_multiplier;
648     }
649 
650     // Ramp up gradually based on speedup_rate
651     double delta = desired_multiplier - state->current_speed_multiplier;
652     state->current_speed_multiplier += delta * cfg->speedup_rate;
653 
654   } else {
655     // Queue is at or below target - slow down to baseline
656     desired_multiplier = cfg->min_speed_multiplier;
657 
658     // Ramp down gradually based on slowdown_rate
659     double delta = desired_multiplier - state->current_speed_multiplier;
660     state->current_speed_multiplier += delta * cfg->slowdown_rate;
661   }
662 
663   // Ensure we stay within configured bounds
664   if (state->current_speed_multiplier < cfg->min_speed_multiplier) {
665     state->current_speed_multiplier = cfg->min_speed_multiplier;
666   }
667   if (state->current_speed_multiplier > cfg->max_speed_multiplier) {
668     state->current_speed_multiplier = cfg->max_speed_multiplier;
669   }
670 
671   // Calculate actual sleep time: baseline / speed_multiplier
672   // Higher multiplier = shorter sleep = faster processing
673   uint64_t sleep_ns = (uint64_t)(cfg->baseline_sleep_ns / state->current_speed_multiplier);
674 
675   // Store for debugging
676   state->last_sleep_ns = sleep_ns;
677 
678   return sleep_ns;
679 }

    adaptive_sleep_state_t::current_speed_multiplier,
    adaptive_sleep_state_t::last_sleep_ns,
    adaptive_sleep_config_t::max_speed_multiplier,
    adaptive_sleep_config_t::min_speed_multiplier,
    adaptive_sleep_config_t::slowdown_rate, and
    adaptive_sleep_config_t::speedup_rate.

int
  format_uptime_hms (int hours, int minutes, int seconds, char * buffer, size_t
  buffer_size)


#include <include/ascii-chat/util/time.h>


Format uptime as HH:MM:SS string.


Parameters


hours Hours component (0-999)


minutes Minutes component (0-59)


seconds Seconds component (0-59)


buffer Output buffer for formatted string


buffer_size Size of output buffer


Returns


Number of characters written (excluding null terminator),
  or -1 on error


Formats an uptime duration as a fixed "HH:MM:SS" string
    with zero-padding. Useful for status displays where consistent formatting is
    preferred over adaptive human-readable formats.




Examples:


    
  
  • format_uptime_hms(0, 0, 5, buf, size) -> "00:00:05"
    • 
  
  • format_uptime_hms(1, 30, 45, buf, size) -> "01:30:45"
    • 
  
  • format_uptime_hms(123, 45, 6, buf, size) -> "123:45:06"
    • 



Note


Buffer should be at least 12 bytes (HH:MM:SS + null
  terminator)



Thread-safe (no global state)




Hours component can exceed 99 (e.g., "123:45:06" for 123
    hours)




Definition at line 354 of file util/time.c.


354                                                                                              {
355   if (!buffer || buffer_size == 0) {
356     return -1;
357   }
358 
359   // Validate components
360   if (hours < 0 || minutes < 0 || minutes >= 60 || seconds < 0 || seconds >= 60) {
361     return -1;
362   }
363 
364   // Format as HH:MM:SS with zero-padding
365   int written = safe_snprintf(buffer, buffer_size, "%02d:%02d:%02d", hours, minutes, seconds);
366 
367   if (written < 0 || (size_t)written >= buffer_size) {
368     return -1;
369   }
370 
371   return written;
372 }

uint64_t time_elapsed_ns (uint64_t start_ns,
  uint64_t end_ns)


#include <include/ascii-chat/util/time.h>


Calculate elapsed time with wraparound safety. Computes the
    difference between two time values, handling potential wraparound of
    uint64_t (which won't happen in practice - uint64_t wraps
    after ~584 years at nanosecond resolution, but this is defensive).




Parameters


start_ns Start time in nanoseconds


end_ns End time in nanoseconds


Returns


Elapsed time in nanoseconds (end - start)


Usage:




uint64_t start = time_get_ns();
// ... do work ...
uint64_t elapsed = time_elapsed_ns(start, time_get_ns());



Definition at line 150 of file util/time.c.


150                                                              {
151   // Handle wraparound (defensive - uint64_t won't wrap in practice at nanosecond resolution)
152   // but this is safe and handles any theoretical edge cases
153   if (end_ns >= start_ns) {
154     return end_ns - start_ns;
155   }
156   // Wraparound case (extremely unlikely)
157   return (UINT64_MAX - start_ns) + end_ns + 1;
158 }

bool time_format_is_valid_strftime (const char *
  format_str)


#include <include/ascii-chat/util/time.h>


Validate strftime format string against known safe specifiers.


Parameters


format_str Format string to validate (e.g.,
  "%H:%M:%S")


Returns


true if valid, false if contains invalid specifiers or
  syntax


Validates format string by checking each % specifier against a
    whitelist of known safe POSIX strftime specifiers. Returns false if:


    
  
  • Contains unterminated % sequences
    • 
  
  • Contains invalid/unsupported specifiers
    • 
  
  • Contains malformed width/precision
    • 
  
  • Contains unsupported locale-dependent specifiers
    • 



Supported specifiers:


    
  
  • Date: Y (4-digit year), m (month), d (day), j (day of year)
    • 
  
  • Date (ISO): F (full date), G (ISO year), g (ISO year short), V (ISO
    week)
    • 
  
  • Time: H (24-hour), M (minute), S (second), I (12-hour), p (AM/PM)
    • 
  
  • Time (combined): T (full time HH:MM:SS), s (seconds since epoch)
    • 
  
  • Locale: a (abbrev weekday), A (full weekday), b (abbrev month), B (full
      month)
    • 
  
  • Locale: c (locale date/time), x (locale date), X (locale time)
    • 
  
  • Timezone: z (offset), Z (name)
    • 
  
  • Weekday: w (0-6), u (1-7)
    • 



Definition at line 699 of file util/time.c.


699                                                            {
700   if (!format_str) {
701     return false;
702   }
703 
704   for (const char *p = format_str; *p; p++) {
705     if (*p == '%') {
706       p++;
707       if (!*p) {
708         /* Unterminated % at end of string */
709         log_error("Invalid time format: unterminated %% at end");
710         return false;
711       }
712 
713       if (*p == '%') {
714         /* Escaped %% - this is valid, just skip it */
715         continue;
716       }
717 
718       /* Optional: flag characters (-, 0, +, space) */
719       if (*p == '-' || *p == '0' || *p == '+' || *p == ' ') {
720         p++;
721         if (!*p) {
722           log_error("Invalid time format: flag character without specifier");
723           return false;
724         }
725       }
726 
727       /* Optional: width specifier (digits or *) */
728       if (*p == '*' || isdigit((unsigned char)*p)) {
729         while (*p && (isdigit((unsigned char)*p) || *p == '*')) {
730           p++;
731         }
732         if (!*p) {
733           log_error("Invalid time format: width specifier without specifier character");
734           return false;
735         }
736       }
737 
738       /* Optional: precision specifier (.digits or .*) */
739       if (*p == '.') {
740         p++;
741         if (!*p) {
742           log_error("Invalid time format: precision specifier incomplete");
743           return false;
744         }
745         while (*p && (isdigit((unsigned char)*p) || *p == '*')) {
746           p++;
747         }
748         if (!*p) {
749           log_error("Invalid time format: precision specifier without specifier character");
750           return false;
751         }
752       }
753 
754       /* Optional: modifier characters (E for %Ex, O for %Ox) */
755       if (*p == 'E' || *p == 'O') {
756         p++;
757         if (!*p) {
758           log_error("Invalid time format: modifier character without specifier");
759           return false;
760         }
761       }
762 
763       /* Validate that the final character is a supported specifier */
764       if (!strchr(SUPPORTED_SPECIFIERS, *p)) {
765         log_error("Invalid time format: unsupported specifier %%%c (at position %ld)", *p, (p - format_str));
766         return false;
767       }
768     }
769   }
770 
771   return true;
772 }

int
  time_format_now (const char * format_str, char * buf, size_t
  buf_size)


#include <include/ascii-chat/util/time.h>


Format current time using strftime format string.


Parameters


format_str strftime format string (should be
  validated with time_format_is_valid_strftime)


buf Output buffer


buf_size Output buffer size


Returns


Number of characters written (excluding null terminator),
  or 0 on error


Formats current wall-clock time (CLOCK_REALTIME) using the
    provided strftime format. Handles nanosecond precision separately: if format
    contains S and buffer has room, appends ".NNNNNN" for microseconds
    (rounded from nanoseconds).




Example outputs:


    
  
  • Format "%H:%M:%S" → "14:30:45.123456"
    • 
  
  • Format "%Y-%m-%d" → "2026-02-16"
    • 
  
  • Format "%F %T" → "2026-02-16
    14:30:45.123456"
    • 



Notes:


    
  
  • Buffer size should be at least 64 bytes
    • 
  
  • Automatic microsecond appending occurs only when S is in format
    • 
  
  • Output is locale-aware (UTF-8 safe from strftime)
    • 
  
  • Returns 0 on error (strftime failure or buffer overflow)
    • 



Definition at line 774 of file util/time.c.


774                                                                         {
775   if (!format_str || !buf || buf_size < 2) {
776     SET_ERRNO(ERROR_INVALID_PARAM, "time_format_now: invalid arguments - format_str=%p, buf=%p, buf_size=%zu",
777               format_str, buf, buf_size);
778     return 0;
779   }
780 
781   /* Get current wall-clock time in nanoseconds */
782   uint64_t ts_ns = time_get_realtime_ns();
783 
784   /* Extract seconds and nanoseconds */
785   time_t seconds = (time_t)(ts_ns / NS_PER_SEC_INT);
786 
787   /* Convert seconds to struct tm */
788   struct tm tm_info;
789   platform_localtime(&seconds, &tm_info);
790 
791   /* Format using strftime */
792   size_t len = strftime(buf, buf_size, format_str, &tm_info);
793 
794   /* Check for strftime errors or buffer overflow */
795   if (len == 0) {
796     /* strftime returned 0 - either error or exact buffer fill */
797     log_debug("strftime returned 0 for format: %s", format_str);
798     return 0;
799   }
800 
801   if (len >= buf_size - 1) {
802     /* Buffer would overflow - strftime filled entire buffer or hit exact size */
803     log_error("time_format_now: buffer too small (need %zu, have %zu)", len + 1, buf_size);
804     return 0;
805   }
806 
807   return (int)len;
808 }

asciichat_error_t time_format_safe (const char *
  format_str, char * buf, size_t buf_size)


#include <include/ascii-chat/util/time.h>


Safe wrapper for time formatting with validation and error
  codes.


Parameters


format_str Format string to use


buf Output buffer


buf_size Output buffer size


Returns


ASCIICHAT_OK on success, error code on failure (includes
  error context)


Validates format string first, then formats time. Returns detailed
    error if:


    
  
  • format_str is NULL
    • 
  
  • buf is NULL
    • 
  
  • buf_size < 64 (minimum safe size)
    • 
  
  • format_str contains invalid specifiers
    • 
  
  • strftime fails
    • 



All errors include context message via SET_ERRNO for
  debugging.




Example:




char timebuf[64];
asciichat_error_t err = time_format_safe("%H:%M:%S", timebuf, sizeof(timebuf));
if (err != ASCIICHAT_OK) {


    log_error("Failed to format time");


    return err;
}
log_info("Current time: %s", timebuf);

811   /* Check NULL pointers */
812   if (!format_str) {
813     return SET_ERRNO(ERROR_INVALID_STATE, "time_format_safe: format_str is NULL");
814   }
815 
816   if (!buf) {
817     return SET_ERRNO(ERROR_INVALID_STATE, "time_format_safe: buf is NULL");
818   }
819 
820   /* Check minimum buffer size */
821   if (buf_size < 64) {
822     return SET_ERRNO(ERROR_INVALID_STATE, "time_format_safe: buffer too small (minimum 64 bytes, got %zu)", buf_size);
823   }
824 
825   /* Validate format string */
826   if (!time_format_is_valid_strftime(format_str)) {
827     return SET_ERRNO(ERROR_INVALID_STATE, "time_format_safe: invalid time format: %s", format_str);
828   }
829 
830   /* Format the time */
831   int result = time_format_now(format_str, buf, buf_size);
832   if (result <= 0) {
833     return SET_ERRNO(ERROR_INVALID_STATE, "time_format_safe: strftime formatting failed");
834   }
835 
836   return ASCIICHAT_OK;
837 }

108                            {
109   // sokol_time provides monotonic clock that never goes backwards
110   // stm_ns() converts ticks to nanoseconds
111   // Ensure sokol_time is initialized before use (defensive programming)
112   if (!g_sokol_time_initialized) {
113     stm_setup();
114     g_sokol_time_initialized = true;
115   }
116   return (uint64_t)stm_ns(stm_now());

uint64_t time_get_realtime_ns (void )


#include <include/ascii-chat/util/time.h>


Get current wall-clock (real) time in nanoseconds. Returns
    high-precision wall-clock time that can jump forwards or backwards when
    system time is adjusted. Use this for timestamps, database records, and
    user-facing time displays.




Returns


Current wall-clock time in nanoseconds
  (CLOCK_REALTIME)


Note: This time can jump backward if system clock is adjusted. For
    measuring elapsed time, use time_get_ns() instead.


Definition at line 119 of file util/time.c.


119                                     {
120 #ifdef _WIN32
121   // Windows: Use GetSystemTimePreciseAsFileTime (Win8+) for high-precision wall clock
122   // FILETIME is 100-nanosecond intervals since January 1, 1601
123   FILETIME ft;
124   GetSystemTimePreciseAsFileTime(&ft);
125   // Convert FILETIME to nanoseconds
126   uint64_t filetime = ((uint64_t)ft.dwHighDateTime << 32) | ft.dwLowDateTime;
127   // Convert 100-nanosecond intervals to nanoseconds
128   return filetime * 100;
129 #else
130   // Get wall-clock (real-time) timestamp
131   struct timespec ts;
132   if (clock_gettime(CLOCK_REALTIME, &ts) != 0) {
133     // Fallback on error (unlikely)
134     return 0;
135   }
136   return time_timespec_to_ns(&ts);
137 #endif
138 }






References time_timespec_to_ns().

int
  time_human_readable (int64_t nanoseconds, char * buffer, size_t
  buffer_size)


#include <include/ascii-chat/util/time.h>


Format nanoseconds as human-readable relative duration (moment.js
    style).


Parameters


nanoseconds Duration in nanoseconds


buffer Output buffer for formatted string


buffer_size Size of output buffer


Returns


Number of characters written (excluding null terminator),
  or -1 on error


Formats a duration using moment.js-compatible thresholds to
    produce natural language relative time strings like "a few seconds
    ago", "3 minutes ago", "2 hours ago", etc.




Threshold table (moment.js compatible):


    
  
  • < 45 s → "a few seconds ago"
    • 
  
  • < 90 s → "a minute ago"
    • 
  
  • < 45 min → "%d minutes ago"
    • 
  
  • < 90 min → "an hour ago"
    • 
  
  • < 22 h → "%d hours ago"
    • 
  
  • < 36 h → "a day ago"
    • 
  
  • < 25 d → "%d days ago"
    • 
  
  • < 45 d → "a month ago"
    • 
  
  • < 11 months → "%d months ago"
    • 
  
  • < 18 months → "a year ago"
    • 
  
  • ≥ 18 months → "%d years ago"
    • 



Note


Buffer should be at least 32 bytes for all possible
  outputs



Thread-safe (no global state)




Format elapsed time as human-readable relative duration with
    past/future support


Parameters


nanoseconds Signed elapsed nanoseconds (negative
  for future times)


buffer Output buffer for formatted string


buffer_size Size of output buffer


Returns


Number of characters written (excluding null terminator),
  or -1 on error


Formats a duration as a human-readable string with
    moment.js-compatible thresholds. Supports both past times ("3 hours
    ago") and future times ("in 5 minutes").




Examples:


    
  
  • Positive: 3600000000000 ns → "an hour ago"
    • 
  
  • Negative: -300000000000 ns → "in 5 minutes"
    • 



Note


Use positive values for elapsed time (past), negative for
  countdown (future)



Buffer should be at least 32 bytes for all possible outputs




Thread-safe (no global state)




Definition at line 572 of file util/time.c.


572                                                                                {
573   if (!buffer || buffer_size == 0) {
574     return -1;
575   }
576 
577   // Determine direction (past vs future)
578   bool is_future = nanoseconds < 0;
579   uint64_t abs_ns = is_future ? (uint64_t)(-nanoseconds) : (uint64_t)nanoseconds;
580 
581   // Convert nanoseconds to seconds for threshold checking
582   double total_seconds = (double)abs_ns / NS_PER_SEC;
583   int minutes = (int)(total_seconds / 60);
584   int hours = minutes / 60;
585   int days = hours / 24;
586   int months = days / 30; // Approximate
587   int years = months / 12;
588 
589   const char *suffix = is_future ? "from now" : "ago";
590   int written =
591       format_human_readable_time_ago(total_seconds, minutes, hours, days, months, years, suffix, buffer, buffer_size);
592 
593   if (written < 0 || (size_t)written >= buffer_size) {
594     return -1;
595   }
596 
597   return written;
598 }






References buffer_size, and NS_PER_SEC.


Referenced by time_human_readable_now().

Parameters

buffer_size Size of output buffer


Returns


Number of characters written (excluding null terminator),

Note


Buffer should be at least 32 bytes for all possible

int
  time_human_readable_unsigned (uint64_t nanoseconds, char * buffer,
  size_t buffer_size)


#include <include/ascii-chat/util/time.h>


Format elapsed time as human-readable relative duration (unsigned
    version).


Parameters


nanoseconds Unsigned elapsed nanoseconds (always
  past times)


buffer Output buffer for formatted string


buffer_size Size of output buffer


Returns


Number of characters written (excluding null terminator),
  or -1 on error


Formats unsigned nanosecond durations as human-readable past-time
    strings. Uses moment.js-compatible thresholds. All values are interpreted as
    elapsed time and formatted with "ago" suffix.




Examples:


    
  
  • 3600000000000 ns → "an hour ago"
    • 
  
  • 300000000000 ns → "5 minutes ago"
    • 



Note


Use this for values from time_get_ns() or other
  unsigned sources



Buffer should be at least 32 bytes for all possible outputs




Thread-safe (no global state)

556   int minutes = (int)(total_seconds / 60);
557   int hours = minutes / 60;
558   int days = hours / 24;
559   int months = days / 30; // Approximate
560   int years = months / 12;
561

563       format_human_readable_time_ago(total_seconds, minutes, hours, days, months, years, "ago", buffer, buffer_size);

int
  time_pretty (uint64_t nanoseconds, int decimals, char * buffer, size_t
  buffer_size)


#include <include/ascii-chat/util/time.h>


Format nanoseconds as pretty duration with spaces and configurable
    precision.


Parameters


nanoseconds Duration in nanoseconds


decimals Number of decimal places (0-9), or -1 for sensible defaults per
  range


buffer Output buffer for formatted string


buffer_size Size of output buffer


Returns


Number of characters written (excluding null terminator),
  or -1 on error


Formats a duration with space-separated number and unit, with
    configurable precision. Automatically strips trailing zeros and decimal
    points.




Examples (with decimals=-1):


    
  
  • 500 ns → "500 ns"
    • 
  
  • 3500 ns → "3.5 µs"
    • 
  
  • 1253000 ns → "1.253 ms"
    • 
  
  • 2500000000 ns → "2.5 s"
    • 
  
  • 83456000000000 ns → "1:23.456" (minutes range,
      MM:SS.fraction)
    • 
  
  • 7530000000000 ns → "2:05:30" (hours range, H:MM:SS)
    • 



Unit selection logic:


    
  
  • [0, 1k) ns → "NNN ns"
    • 
  
  • [1k, 1M) ns → "N.N µs" (up to 3 decimals)
    • 
  
  • [1M, 1G) ns → "N.NNN ms" (up to 3 decimals)
    • 
  
  • [1G, 60G) ns → "N.NN s" (up to 2 decimals)
    • 
  
  • [60G, 3.6T) ns → "M:SS.fraction" (colon notation, up to 3
      decimals)
    • 
  
  • [3.6T+) ns → "H:MM:SS" (colon notation)
    • 



Note


Buffer should be at least 32 bytes for all possible
  outputs



Thread-safe (no global state)




decimals=-1 uses appropriate defaults (3 for
    ns/µs/ms/colon, 2 for s)




decimals=0 produces no decimal point




Definition at line 424 of file util/time.c.


424                                                                                       {
425   if (!buffer || buffer_size == 0) {
426     return -1;
427   }
428 
429   // Handle zero
430   if (nanoseconds == 0) {
431     int written = safe_snprintf(buffer, buffer_size, "0 ns");
432     return (written < 0 || (size_t)written >= buffer_size) ? -1 : written;
433   }
434 
435   // Determine decimals for this range if not explicitly set
436   int actual_decimals = decimals;
437   if (decimals == -1) {
438     if (nanoseconds < NS_PER_MIN_INT) {
439       actual_decimals = (nanoseconds < NS_PER_SEC_INT) ? 3 : 2; // ns/µs/ms or seconds
440     } else {
441       actual_decimals = 3; // colon notation
442     }
443   }
444 
445   // Clamp decimals to valid range
446   if (actual_decimals < 0)
447     actual_decimals = 0;
448   if (actual_decimals > 9)
449     actual_decimals = 9;
450 
451   int written = 0;
452 
453   // Nanoseconds (< 1µs)
454   if (nanoseconds < NS_PER_US_INT) {
455     written = safe_snprintf(buffer, buffer_size, "%llu ns", (unsigned long long)nanoseconds);
456   }
457   // Microseconds (< 1ms)
458   else if (nanoseconds < NS_PER_MS_INT) {
459     double us = (double)nanoseconds / NS_PER_US;
460     char temp_buf[64];
461     // Use a direct snprintf with large enough buffer first
462     snprintf(temp_buf, sizeof(temp_buf), "%.*f µs", actual_decimals, us);
463     strip_trailing_zeros(temp_buf);
464     written = (int)strlen(temp_buf);
465     if (written > 0 && written < (int)buffer_size) {
466       SAFE_STRNCPY(buffer, temp_buf, buffer_size);
467     } else {
468       written = -1;
469     }
470   }
471   // Milliseconds (< 1s)
472   else if (nanoseconds < NS_PER_SEC_INT) {
473     double ms = (double)nanoseconds / NS_PER_MS;
474     char temp_buf[64];
475     snprintf(temp_buf, sizeof(temp_buf), "%.*f ms", actual_decimals, ms);
476     strip_trailing_zeros(temp_buf);
477     written = (int)strlen(temp_buf);
478     if (written > 0 && written < (int)buffer_size) {
479       SAFE_STRNCPY(buffer, temp_buf, buffer_size);
480     } else {
481       written = -1;
482     }
483   }
484   // Seconds (< 1 minute)
485   else if (nanoseconds < NS_PER_MIN_INT) {
486     double s = (double)nanoseconds / NS_PER_SEC;
487     char temp_buf[64];
488     snprintf(temp_buf, sizeof(temp_buf), "%.*f s", actual_decimals, s);
489     strip_trailing_zeros(temp_buf);
490     written = (int)strlen(temp_buf);
491     if (written > 0 && written < (int)buffer_size) {
492       SAFE_STRNCPY(buffer, temp_buf, buffer_size);
493     } else {
494       written = -1;
495     }
496   }
497   // Minutes (< 1 hour) - use colon notation: M:SS.frac
498   else if (nanoseconds < NS_PER_HOUR_INT) {
499     uint64_t total_seconds = nanoseconds / NS_PER_SEC_INT;
500     uint64_t minutes = total_seconds / 60;
501     uint64_t seconds = total_seconds % 60;
502     uint64_t remainder_ns = nanoseconds % NS_PER_SEC_INT;
503     double fraction = (double)remainder_ns / NS_PER_SEC;
504 
505     char temp_buf[64];
506     if (actual_decimals == 0) {
507       snprintf(temp_buf, sizeof(temp_buf), "%llu:%02llu", (unsigned long long)minutes, (unsigned long long)seconds);
508     } else {
509       snprintf(temp_buf, sizeof(temp_buf), "%llu:%02llu.%.*f", (unsigned long long)minutes, (unsigned long long)seconds,
510                actual_decimals, fraction);
511       strip_trailing_zeros(temp_buf);
512     }
513 
514     written = (int)strlen(temp_buf);
515     if (written > 0 && written < (int)buffer_size) {
516       SAFE_STRNCPY(buffer, temp_buf, buffer_size);
517     } else {
518       written = -1;
519     }
520   }
521   // Hours+ - use colon notation: H:MM:SS or HH:MM:SS
522   else {
523     uint64_t total_seconds = nanoseconds / NS_PER_SEC_INT;
524     uint64_t hours = total_seconds / 3600;
525     uint64_t remaining = total_seconds % 3600;
526     uint64_t minutes = remaining / 60;
527     uint64_t seconds = remaining % 60;
528 
529     written = safe_snprintf(buffer, buffer_size, "%llu:%02llu:%02llu", (unsigned long long)hours,
530                             (unsigned long long)minutes, (unsigned long long)seconds);
531   }
532 
533   if (written < 0 || (size_t)written >= buffer_size) {
534     return -1;
535   }
536 
537   return written;
538 }

void
  time_sleep_ns (uint64_t ns)


#include <include/ascii-chat/util/time.h>


Sleep for specified nanoseconds. Sleeps the current thread for at
    least the specified number of nanoseconds. On some systems, actual sleep
    time may be slightly longer due to scheduler granularity.




Parameters


ns Number of nanoseconds to sleep

140                                 {
141   // Use platform abstraction for cross-platform sleep
142   // Convert nanoseconds to microseconds (1 us = 1000 ns)
143   unsigned int usec = (unsigned int)(ns / 1000);
144   if (usec == 0 && ns > 0) {
145     usec = 1; // Minimum 1 microsecond
146   }
147   platform_sleep_us(usec);
148 }

164                                                         {
165   if (!ts) {
166     SET_ERRNO(ERROR_INVALID_PARAM, "null ts");
167     return 0;
168   }
169   // Use __uint128_t to prevent overflow during multiplication of large time values
170   __uint128_t result = (__uint128_t)ts->tv_sec * NS_PER_SEC_INT;
171   if (result > UINT64_MAX) {
172     SET_ERRNO(ERROR_INVALID_PARAM, "time_timespec_to_ns: timespec value would overflow");
173     return UINT64_MAX;
174   }
175   return (uint64_t)result + (uint64_t)ts->tv_nsec;
176 }

bool timer_start (const char * name)


#include <include/ascii-chat/util/time.h>


Start a named timer. Creates a new timer record and stores the
    start time. If a timer with the same name exists, it will be
  overwritten.




Parameters


name Timer name (must be unique, will be
  copied)


Returns


true on success, false on failure


Definition at line 240 of file util/time.c.


240                                    {
241   if (!lifecycle_is_initialized(&g_timer_manager.lifecycle)) {
242     SET_ERRNO(ERROR_INVALID_STATE, "Timer system not initialized");
243     return false;
244   }
245 
246   if (!name) {
247     SET_ERRNO(ERROR_INVALID_PARAM, "Timer name is NULL");
248     return false;
249   }
250 
251   rwlock_wrlock(&g_timer_manager.rwlock);
252 
253   // Check if timer already exists - this is an error, timers must be unique
254   timer_record_t *existing = NULL;
255   HASH_FIND_STR(g_timer_manager.timers, name, existing);
256 
257   if (existing) {
258     rwlock_wrunlock(&g_timer_manager.rwlock);
259     SET_ERRNO(ERROR_INVALID_STATE, "Timer '%s' already exists - timers must have unique names", name);
260     return false;
261   }
262 
263   // Create new timer record
264   timer_record_t *timer = SAFE_MALLOC(sizeof(timer_record_t), timer_record_t *);
265   if (!timer) {
266     rwlock_wrunlock(&g_timer_manager.rwlock);
267     SET_ERRNO(ERROR_MEMORY, "Failed to allocate timer record");
268     return false;
269   }
270 
271   // Use platform-safe string duplication
272   size_t name_len = strlen(name) + 1;
273   timer->name = SAFE_MALLOC(name_len, char *);
274   if (!timer->name) {
275     SAFE_FREE(timer);
276     rwlock_wrunlock(&g_timer_manager.rwlock);
277     SET_ERRNO(ERROR_MEMORY, "Failed to allocate timer name");
278     return false;
279   }
280   SAFE_STRNCPY(timer->name, name, name_len);
281 
282   timer->start_ticks = stm_now();
283 
284   // Add to hashtable
285   HASH_ADD_KEYPTR(hh, g_timer_manager.timers, timer->name, strlen(timer->name), timer);
286 
287   rwlock_wrunlock(&g_timer_manager.rwlock);
288   return true;
289 }

    and timer_record::start_ticks.






double
  timer_stop (const char * name)


#include <include/ascii-chat/util/time.h>


Stop a named timer and return elapsed time. Looks up the timer by
    name, calculates elapsed time, logs it, and removes the timer from the
    hashtable.




Parameters


name Timer name to stop


Returns


Elapsed time in nanoseconds, or -1.0 if timer not
  found


Definition at line 291 of file util/time.c.


291                                     {
292   if (!lifecycle_is_initialized(&g_timer_manager.lifecycle)) {
293     SET_ERRNO(ERROR_INVALID_STATE, "Timer system not initialized");
294     return -1.0;
295   }
296 
297   if (!name) {
298     SET_ERRNO(ERROR_INVALID_PARAM, "Timer name is NULL");
299     return -1.0;
300   }
301 
302   rwlock_wrlock(&g_timer_manager.rwlock);
303 
304   // Find timer
305   timer_record_t *timer = NULL;
306   HASH_FIND_STR(g_timer_manager.timers, name, timer);
307 
308   if (!timer) {
309     rwlock_wrunlock(&g_timer_manager.rwlock);
310     log_dev("Timer '%s' not found (may have already been stopped)", name);
311     return -1.0;
312   }
313 
314   // Calculate elapsed time in nanoseconds for maximum precision
315   uint64_t end_ticks = stm_now();
316   uint64_t elapsed_ticks = stm_diff(end_ticks, timer->start_ticks);
317   double elapsed_ns = stm_ns(elapsed_ticks);
318 
319   // Format duration for human-readable logging
320   char duration_str[32];
321   time_pretty((uint64_t)elapsed_ns, -1, duration_str, sizeof(duration_str));
322 
323   // Log the result (dev level - only shown with --verbose)
324   log_dev("Timer '%s': %s", name, duration_str);
325 
326   // Save pointers to free BEFORE removing from hashtable
327   char *timer_name = timer->name;
328 
329   // Remove from hashtable (HASH_DEL only removes from hash, doesn't free memory)
330   HASH_DEL(g_timer_manager.timers, timer);
331 
332   // Free timer->name first (this was allocated separately)
333   SAFE_FREE(timer_name);
334 
335   // Nullify the timer->name field so debug_free doesn't try to access it
336   // when freeing the timer structure itself
337   timer->name = NULL;
338 
339   // Free the timer record itself
340   SAFE_FREE(timer);
341 
342   rwlock_wrunlock(&g_timer_manager.rwlock);
343   return elapsed_ns; // Return nanoseconds
344 }






References ERROR_INVALID_PARAM, ERROR_INVALID_STATE,
    lifecycle_is_initialized(), log_dev,
    timer_record::name, rwlock_wrlock, rwlock_wrunlock,
    SAFE_FREE, SET_ERRNO, timer_record::start_ticks, and
    time_pretty().






void
  timer_system_destroy (void )


#include <include/ascii-chat/util/time.h>


Cleanup the timing system. Frees all timer records and destroys
    mutex. Should be called at program exit.


Definition at line 213 of file util/time.c.


213                                 {
214   if (!lifecycle_is_initialized(&g_timer_manager.lifecycle)) {
215     return;
216   }
217 
218   rwlock_wrlock(&g_timer_manager.rwlock);
219 
220   // Free all timer records
221   int timer_count = 0;
222   timer_record_t *current, *tmp;
223   HASH_ITER(hh, g_timer_manager.timers, current, tmp) {
224     HASH_DEL(g_timer_manager.timers, current);
225     SAFE_FREE(current->name);
226     SAFE_FREE(current);
227     timer_count++;
228   }
229 
230   g_timer_manager.timers = NULL;
231 
232   rwlock_wrunlock(&g_timer_manager.rwlock);
233 
234   /* Shutdown lifecycle and destroy rwlock together */
235   lifecycle_shutdown(&g_timer_manager.lifecycle);
236 
237   log_debug("Timer system cleaned up (freed %d timers)", timer_count);
238 }






References lifecycle_is_initialized(),
    lifecycle_shutdown(), log_debug, timer_record::name,
    rwlock_wrlock, rwlock_wrunlock, and SAFE_FREE.

bool timer_system_init (void )


#include <include/ascii-chat/util/time.h>


Initialize the timing system. Must be called before using any
    timer functions. Automatically calls stm_setup() for sokol_time
    initialization.




Returns


true on success, false on failure


Definition at line 191 of file util/time.c.


191                              {
192   if (lifecycle_is_initialized(&g_timer_manager.lifecycle)) {
193     return true;
194   }
195 
196   // Initialize sokol_time
197   stm_setup();
198 
199   // Initialize rwlock and lifecycle together
200   g_timer_manager.lifecycle.sync_type = LIFECYCLE_SYNC_RWLOCK;
201   g_timer_manager.lifecycle.sync.rwlock = &g_timer_manager.rwlock;
202   if (!lifecycle_init(&g_timer_manager.lifecycle, "timer_manager")) {
203     SET_ERRNO(ERROR_PLATFORM_INIT, "Failed to initialize timer system (already initialized by another thread)");
204     return true; // Another thread already initialized it
205   }
206 
207   g_timer_manager.timers = NULL;
208 
209   log_dev("Timer system initialized");
210   return true;
211 }
